content.text: Traditional web service incidents fit a simple pattern: the service is either reachable or it is not, and the error rate tells you which. GenAI services can fail across five independent dimensions simultaneously — a request can succeed at the HTTP layer, return a valid JSON response, stay within latency SLOs, and still constitute a high-severity incident because the output is harmful, hallucinatory, or causes a downstream compliance violation. A taxonomy that does not capture this breadth will produce mis-classified incidents and wrong runbooks.

The taxonomy below defines five failure categories, each with its own detection source, severity default, and resolution path. A single production incident may span multiple categories (for example, a RAG pipeline failure that degrades output quality
exposes a latency SLO breach), but each category should be tracked independently because the remediation steps differ.

attrs.alt: AI Service Failure Taxonomy — five categories (Availability, Output Quality, Safety/Compliance, Resource/Cost, Data/Context) with leaf failure types and detection sources

content.text: Availability failures are the closest to traditional web service incidents — the model API is unreachable, timed out, or returning 5xx errors at a rate that breaks the user experience. They are the easiest category to detect (error rate alerts fire within seconds) but present a GenAI-specific twist: the root cause may lie entirely outside the engineering team's control (provider outage) or entirely inside it (a deploy that misconfigured the API client).
— the provider endpoint returns 5xx or connection refused. Detection:
sustained for 2 minutes. Immediate action: activate the fallback model or queue requests if the operation is asynchronous.

— the application's circuit breaker trips because the provider error rate crossed the threshold. The user sees a graceful-degradation response (cached answer, stub, or 'service temporarily unavailable'). This is not a user-impacting outage if the fallback is working, but the circuit-open state should still page the on-call because it signals a provider-side problem.
— the provider's per-minute or per-day token quota is exhausted, returning 429s. Distinct from soft throttling (intermittent 429s that back-off successfully). Detection:
for more than 5 minutes. Resolution: emergency quota increase request to the provider + route lower-priority traffic to a secondary model.

# Prometheus alert: API availability
- alert: GenAIAvailabilityDegraded
  expr: |
    (
      sum(rate(llm_request_errors_total{error_type=~"5xx|timeout|connection_refused"}[5m]))
      /
      sum(rate(llm_requests_total[5m]))
    ) > 0.05
  for: 2m
  labels:
    severity: sev2     # escalates to sev1 if > 0.5 or all_users scope
  annotations:
    summary: "LLM API error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} exceeds 5%"
    runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/genai/availability"

Output quality failures are invisible to infrastructure monitoring — the HTTP layer shows 200s, latency is normal, tokens flow — but the content the model returns has degraded to the point where it hurts users. This category is the most important GenAI-specific addition to any incident taxonomy because it has no analog in traditional web services.

Hallucination occurs when the model confidently produces information that is false. It can emerge suddenly after a provider model upgrade (the new model version has different calibration) or gradually due to prompt template changes that reduce the model's access to grounding context. Detection requires an automated evaluation harness that checks model outputs against a curated set of ground-truth questions on each deployment and periodically in production.

An eval score regression is a sustained drop in the aggregate quality metric from your continuous evaluation pipeline — not a single bad response but a distribution shift. The standard threshold is a drop of more than 10% from the 7-day rolling baseline, sustained for 15 minutes or more. This triggers a SEV 3 (subset of users affected) or SEV 2 (platform-wide) depending on scope.
Provider silently rolled out a new model checkpoint without changing the model ID
Prompt template change that shortened the system prompt and removed key grounding instructions
Retrieval pipeline changes that reduced context relevance (see Data/Context category)
Temperature or sampling parameter changes in a config deploy

Many GenAI services rely on structured outputs — JSON, XML, or a specific markdown schema. When the model stops following the output schema (either because of a prompt regression or a model update that changed behavior on constrained generation), downstream parsers throw exceptions. Detection:
on the response processor, or a spike in

from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError
import anthropic, json

class ProductRecommendation(BaseModel):
    product_id: str
    reason: str
    confidence: float   # 0.0–1.0

def extract_recommendation(user_query: str) -> ProductRecommendation | None:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        system="You are a product recommender. Always respond with valid JSON matching the schema: {product_id, reason, confidence}",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_query}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text
    try:
        data = json.loads(raw)
        return ProductRecommendation(**data)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValidationError) as exc:
        # Increment schema_validation_failures_total counter here
        # This rate is your format-failure incident signal
        print(f"Schema failure: {exc}")
        return None
Safety and compliance failures are the only incident category that

escalate to SEV 1, regardless of scope. A single user receiving harmful output is a SEV 1 incident. A single PII leak is a SEV 1 incident. There is no 'only a small number of users affected' mitigating factor for this category because the regulatory and reputational consequences of inaction are disproportionate to the user-count dimension.

— the model produces content that violates the application's safety policy: instructions for dangerous activities, hate speech, misleading medical/legal advice, or any content prohibited by the platform's terms of service. Detection: an inline classifier scores every response; a safety flag on any response triggers an incident.

— the model returns information from one user's context (or from training data) in another user's response. Often caused by a context assembly bug (shared memory or vector store not properly scoped to the user) rather than a model defect. Detection: PII detection regex on all responses; entity cross-referencing in multi-turn sessions.

— a malicious user-supplied input hijacks the model's behavior, causing it to ignore system instructions, exfiltrate context, or perform actions outside its intended scope. Detection: output monitoring for instruction-override phrases; anomaly detection on response patterns relative to system-prompt intent.

SEV 1 — Safety Override: When a safety or compliance failure is detected, disable the affected feature immediately, preserve the full request/response pair as evidence, notify legal within 30 minutes, and do not wait for root-cause analysis before disabling. Restoration requires explicit sign-off from the safety and legal teams, not just engineering.

GenAI services have a fundamentally different cost profile from traditional web services: a single LLM call can cost 100× more than a typical API call, and the cost scales with request content, not just request count. A runaway prompt template that doubles token usage, or a feature that unexpectedly goes viral, can exhaust a monthly budget in hours.

A cost explosion is when the actual hourly spend rate exceeds the projected hourly rate by a factor of 2× or more. It is a resource incident even if the service is functioning correctly from a user's perspective. Detection requires tracking
from the OTel semantic conventions and converting them to cost using the provider's per-token pricing.

from prometheus_client import Counter, Gauge

INPUT_TOKENS  = Counter("llm_input_tokens_total",  "Cumulative input tokens",  ["model", "feature"])
OUTPUT_TOKENS = Counter("llm_output_tokens_total", "Cumulative output tokens", ["model", "feature"])
COST_GAUGE    = Gauge("llm_estimated_cost_usd",    "Estimated cost this hour", ["model"])

# Per-million-token pricing (update as provider pricing changes)
PRICE_PER_1M = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001":    {"input": 0.80,  "output": 4.00},
    "claude-sonnet-5":              {"input": 3.00,  "output": 15.00},
    "gpt-4o":                       {"input": 2.50,  "output": 10.00},
}

def record_llm_usage(model: str, feature: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    INPUT_TOKENS.labels(model=model, feature=feature).inc(input_tokens)
    OUTPUT_TOKENS.labels(model=model, feature=feature).inc(output_tokens)
    price = PRICE_PER_1M.get(model, {"input": 3.0, "output": 15.0})
    cost  = (input_tokens / 1_000_000 * price["input"]
             + output_tokens / 1_000_000 * price["output"])
    COST_GAUGE.labels(model=model).inc(cost)
    return cost

When a provider enforces a requests-per-minute (RPM) or tokens-per-minute (TPM) quota, a traffic spike can exhaust the quota and produce 429 errors. If the retry logic uses fixed intervals instead of exponential backoff with jitter, all retry attempts arrive at the provider simultaneously, producing a thundering-herd cascade. The application appears to be experiencing an outage but the root cause is exhausted quota with a self-inflicted retry storm.

Most production GenAI applications are not bare LLM wrappers — they assemble context from retrieval pipelines, databases, document stores, or external APIs before constructing the prompt. Data and context failures occur when this assembly pipeline malfunctions, producing prompts that contain stale, incorrect, or maliciously-injected context.

A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline failure manifests as a quality regression, not an availability incident — the model still responds, but it is now responding without correct grounding context. The characteristic signal is a sudden increase in answers like 'I don't have information about that' or a drop in retrieval hit rate from the vector store.

from opentelemetry import trace
import time

tracer = trace.get_tracer("rag.pipeline")

def retrieve_context(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("rag.retrieve") as span:
        start = time.monotonic()
        results = vector_store.similarity_search(query, k=top_k)  # type: ignore
        latency = time.monotonic() - start

        span.set_attribute("rag.query", query[:200])
        span.set_attribute("rag.results_count", len(results))
        span.set_attribute("rag.latency_ms", latency * 1000)
        # Track top score — a drop below 0.7 often signals embedding drift
        if results:
            span.set_attribute("rag.top_score", results[0].get("score", 0))
        return results

# Alert if retrieval hit rate (results_count > 0) drops below 80%
# or if top_score drops below 0.5 for 10+ consecutive queries

Context poisoning occurs when malicious or incorrect data enters the retrieval corpus and gets injected into the model's context window. Unlike prompt injection (where the adversarial content comes from the user's input), context poisoning originates from data ingested into the knowledge base itself — a compromised document, a scraped page with embedded adversarial text, or an incorrectly processed chunk that contains instructions rather than factual content.
Defense in depth for context poisoning:
Sanitize retrieved chunks before insertion into the prompt: strip markdown headings that look like system prompt sections (
Embed retrieval content in a clearly delimited section:
— this makes it structurally clear to the model that the enclosed content is data, not instructions
Monitor for anomalous chunks: run retrieved text through a classifier that flags instruction-like patterns before the chunk reaches the model
Audit the ingestion pipeline: any external document ingestion should go through content review before entering the retrieval corpus

Embedding drift occurs when the embedding model used to encode queries differs from the model used to encode the stored documents, or when either model is updated, causing the semantic space to shift. The vector index built with the old embeddings becomes misaligned with queries encoded with the new model, and retrieval quality silently degrades.
Detection: compute the cosine similarity between a held-out set of canonical query-document pairs on each embedding model deployment. A drop below 0.85 indicates drift requiring re-indexing.

import numpy as np

def check_embedding_drift(
    canonical_pairs: list[tuple[str, str]],
    embed_fn,
    drift_threshold: float = 0.85,
) -> dict:
    """
    canonical_pairs: list of (query, expected_top_document) tuples
    embed_fn: function(text) -> np.ndarray
    """
    similarities = []
    for query, doc in canonical_pairs:
        q_vec  = embed_fn(query)
        d_vec  = embed_fn(doc)
        cosine = np.dot(q_vec, d_vec) / (np.linalg.norm(q_vec) * np.linalg.norm(d_vec))
        similarities.append(float(cosine))
    mean_sim = float(np.mean(similarities))
    return {
        "mean_cosine_similarity": mean_sim,
        "drift_detected": mean_sim < drift_threshold,
        "n_pairs": len(canonical_pairs),
    }
The following table maps each incident type to its default severity, primary detection signal, and immediate mitigation step. Use this as the first page of each incident type's runbook.
When an incident spans multiple categories, apply these rules:
— if any safety signal fires, classify as Category 3 (Safety) SEV 1 regardless of which other category the root cause falls under.
Use the most severe applicable category

— an outage that is also causing quality degradation is classified as an Availability incident (the higher-visibility signal that users are experiencing directly), but both categories are tracked in the incident record.
Root cause determines the resolution runbook
, not the user-facing symptom. A quality degradation caused by a RAG pipeline failure runs the Data/Context runbook, not the Output Quality runbook, even though it presents as a quality incident.
Classify by observed state, not hypothesized cause
— at incident declaration time you may not know the root cause. Classify by what you can measure. Reclassify in the post-mortem if the root cause changes the category.

The five-category taxonomy — Availability, Output Quality, Safety/Compliance, Resource/Cost, and Data/Context — gives incident responders a structured first-step for classifying GenAI failures. The critical distinctions from traditional incident taxonomies are: (1) quality and safety failures are first-class incident types even when the service is operationally healthy; (2) safety/compliance failures always escalate to SEV 1 and require legal notification; (3) data-pipeline failures produce quality incidents with a different runbook from model-tier quality regressions. Each category has its own detection source and resolution playbook — mapping the signal to the correct category is the first, most time-sensitive step in incident response.
